description: baseline (SwiGLU d16, partial RoPE, value embeddings, token shifting, QK-norm, SSSL window pattern, Muon+AdamW, WSD 5% warmup + 95% stable, muP base=768, MXFP8, 222M params, 200M tokens on ClimbMix)

Differential attention: pair 6 heads into 3 diff heads (y1-sigmoid(lambda)*y2 with lambda init 0.8) + head RMSNorm + c_proj 384->768 (hypothesis: subtracting paired attention outputs cancels noise per ICLR2025 paper arxiv:2410.05258; evidence: modded-nanogpt community gains): val_bpb 0.919->0.925 WORSE. Conclusion: halving attention output dim from 768 to 384 before c_proj reduces model capacity more than noise cancellation helps. Simple output-pairing version of diff attention loses too much expressivity at 6-head scale. Need full formulation (4 SDPA calls to preserve output dim) or more heads.

Remove MLP-only layers: set MLP_ONLY_LAYERS={} so all 16 layers have attention+MLP (hypothesis: 3 MLP-only layers waste capacity since throughput irrelevant with fixed token budget; evidence: 3.9GB unused VRAM): val_bpb 0.919->0.923 WORSE. Added 7M params (222->229M) and 3 more attention layers (all short-window S-layers). Conclusion: baseline attention/MLP balance was already optimal at this depth. Short-window attention in layers 12-14 adds compute without quality benefit. MLP-only layers were NOT a bottleneck.

Increase model width 768->896 (ASPECT_RATIO 48->56, 7 heads, 281M params) (hypothesis: wider model extracts more per token due to scaling laws; evidence: 3.9GB unused VRAM headroom, heavily undertrained at 1 tok/param): val_bpb 0.919->0.922 WORSE despite 27% more params and better training loss (2.685 vs 2.709). MFU improved 71->78%. Conclusion: at 200M token budget, 281M params is OVER-parameterized. The model doesn't have enough data to overcome the optimization difficulty of the larger model with only 1526 steps. Optimal model size for 200M tokens is closer to 222M (current baseline). Scaling width doesn't help without more tokens.

Increase MATRIX_LR 0.04->0.06 (+50% Muon hidden layer LR, c_proj 0.08->0.12) (hypothesis: higher LR speeds up learning from 200M tokens; evidence: 3 failed arch experiments suggest model capacity is optimal but training could be faster): val_bpb 0.919->0.934 MUCH WORSE. Final train loss similar (2.725 vs 2.709) but val_bpb degraded by 0.015. Heavy loss oscillations (2.6-3.1) during stable phase indicate LR too high. Conclusion: MATRIX_LR=0.04 is already near-optimal. Higher LR causes instability that destroys generalization. Future: try LOWER LR or change other hyperparams.

Attention output gating: per-head sigmoid gate on SDPA output (gate=2*sigmoid(Linear(x[:,:,:12])) in [0,2], init 1.0 identity) (hypothesis: gate reduces attention sink on BOS/special tokens per NeurIPS 2025 best paper by Qwen; evidence: modded-nanogpt PR#117 -55 steps): val_bpb 0.919->0.920 barely worse. Tiny 936 new params. Train loss identical (2.708 vs 2.709). Conclusion: baseline already handles attention sinks well via QK-norm+logit softcap+ve_gate. Additional output gating provides no benefit at this scale/token budget. Gate may need more tokens to learn useful patterns.

Remove weight decay (WEIGHT_DECAY 0.1->0.0) (hypothesis: at 200M tokens / 222M params the model is heavily undertrained, WD constrains optimization unnecessarily; evidence: 5 failed experiments show baseline well-tuned but no WD experiments tried, cautious WD+linear decay already very conservative): val_bpb 0.919->0.908 MAJOR IMPROVEMENT of 0.011! Train loss also better (2.681 vs 2.709). Conclusion: weight decay is harmful at this token budget. The model is so data-starved that any parameter regularization hurts learning. This is consistent with 1-token-per-param regime where overfitting is impossible.

U-net skip connections: add skip from early layers to mirror late layers (hypothesis: improved gradient flow from modded-nanogpt architecture; evidence: proven in speedrun): training completed 1525 steps (final loss 2.707 similar to baseline 2.681) but eval crashed due to torch.compile+activation_checkpointing interaction in eval mode. Re-run stuck in compilation for 30+ min. Conclusion: U-net skips incompatible with current torch.compile setup; would need compile-friendly implementation.

ReLU² activation in MLP (SwiGLU->ReGLU²: F.silu->F.relu().square()) (hypothesis: sparser activations improve feature selection in undertrained regime; evidence: modded-nanogpt uses ReLU² as standard at 124M scale): val_bpb 0.908->0.910 WORSE by 0.0025. Train loss similar (2.678 vs 2.681). MFU slightly lower (71.8 vs 73.4). Conclusion: SiLU (smooth gating) outperforms ReLU² (hard sparse gating) at 222M params / 200M tokens. The smooth gradient of SiLU likely helps optimization in this undertrained regime where every gradient step matters.

Logit softcap 15->30 (hypothesis: less logit regularization helps in undertrained regime like WD=0 success; evidence: WD removal gave 0.011 gain): val_bpb 0.908->0.948 MUCH WORSE (+0.040). Train loss similar (2.709) but val_bpb catastrophically worse. Conclusion: softcap=15 is NOT just regularization - it shapes the loss landscape fundamentally. With softcap=30 the tanh gradient near zero is ~1.0 (nearly linear) so softcap barely activates. The tight softcap=15 compresses logits into a well-conditioned range that helps optimization. Unlike WD which constrains param magnitude softcap constrains output scale - critical for stable cross-entropy training.

HEAD_DIM 128->64 (6->12 attention heads same 768 dim) (hypothesis: more heads = more diverse attention patterns helps in undertrained regime; evidence: diff attention exp concluded 'need more heads'): val_bpb 0.908->0.928 WORSE (+0.020). Train loss similar (2.697 vs 2.681). MFU slightly lower (74.0 vs 73.4). Conclusion: at 222M params 64 head_dim is too small. With partial RoPE (50%) only 16 dims get positional encoding per head (vs 32 with 128 head_dim) - insufficient for learning token positions. Also tanh softcap=15 constrains logits - with more heads the logit composition is more distributed which may interact badly. 6x128 gives better per-head expressivity than 12x64 at this scale.

SHORT_WINDOW 256->512 (wider banded attention for S-layers) (hypothesis: wider context window helps short-window attention layers; evidence: nanochat autoresearch found 'banded attention was too conservative'): val_bpb 0.908->0.911 WORSE (+0.004). Train loss identical (2.680 vs 2.681). MFU same (73.4 vs 73.4). tok/sec slightly lower (46.4k vs 49.4k) due to wider flex_attention blocks. Conclusion: at 200M tokens wider windows add compute without quality benefit. The SSSL pattern with L-layers every 4th already provides sufficient global context. Short-window layers are meant for efficient local attention - widening them wastes compute on distant tokens that L-layers already cover. The nanochat finding may apply to different window patterns or deeper models.

Learnable attention temperature per-layer (exp(attn_temp)*q scaling init=0 lr=scalar_lr*0.1) (hypothesis: QKnorm makes attention too diffuse learnable temp sharpens it; evidence: nanochat autoresearch found QKnorm scaler multiplier was missing): val_bpb 0.908->0.910 WORSE by 0.002. Train loss identical (2.686 vs 2.681). MFU lower (71.5 vs 73.4) due to extra exp() computation. Conclusion: at 222M/200M tokens the default QK-norm + 1/sqrt(d) scaling is already near-optimal for this model. Adding learnable temperature adds 16 scalar params but the model can't benefit from sharper attention at this scale. Nanochat finding may require specific init or schedule we didn't replicate. Close to noise threshold but below 0.002 improvement bar.

value_emb weight decay 0.0->0.1 (VE-only WD) (hypothesis: value embeddings benefit from regularization even when other params don't; evidence: nanochat autoresearch found 'Value Embeddings really like regularization and none was being applied'): val_bpb 0.908->0.938 MUCH WORSE (+0.031). Train loss worse too (2.724 vs 2.681). Conclusion: in our architecture VE serves a different role (injected via learned gate into attention v). Adding WD to VE prevents it from developing strong features that provide context through the attention mechanism. The nanochat VE architecture may be different (direct concat not gated injection). Our WD=0 across all params is optimal - the finding from nanochat doesn't transfer because of architectural differences in how VE integrates.

WARMUP_RATIO 0.05->0.02 (shorter warmup from 76->30 steps) (hypothesis: faster ramp-up gives more productive full-LR training steps in undertrained regime; evidence: WD removal success shows less constraint helps): val_bpb 0.908->0.913 WORSE (+0.006). Train loss nearly identical (2.683 vs 2.681). Conclusion: the 5% warmup is actually doing important work stabilizing the early training phase. Faster ramp-up degrades generalization even though training loss is similar. The Muon optimizer with orthogonalization still needs adequate warmup for embedding/lm_head params that use AdamW. Shorter warmup = worse final val_bpb consistently.

DEPTH 16->20 keeping width 768 constant (ASPECT_RATIO 48->38 adds 4 more attn+MLP layers at idx 15-19 250M params) (hypothesis: depth improves composition/hierarchy independently of width; evidence: width exp fd207b7 scaled dim+heads to 281M and failed but depth is fundamentally different adding sequential compositional processing not just capacity; research openreview 'Impact of Depth and Width' shows depth specifically improves OOD generalization): val_bpb 0.9076->0.8988 MAJOR IMPROVEMENT 0.0087. Final train loss 2.662 vs baseline 2.681. Train took 83min (exceeds 75min budget). Conclusion: depth is NOT the same lever as width in undertrained regime - adding sequential layers helps but increasing per-layer capacity hurts. Sequential composition matters more than per-layer capacity at 200M tokens.

DEPTH 20->22 (width 768 constant ASPECT_RATIO 38->34 adds 2 more attn+MLP layers 264M params) (hypothesis: depth scaling continues to help since d16->d20 gave +0.0087; evidence: immediately prior depth exp keep): val_bpb 0.8988->0.8988 FLAT (delta -0.00002 pure noise). Final train loss 2.659 similar to d20's 2.662. Conclusion: depth scaling SATURATES at d20 for this token budget+width combination. Adding 2 more layers provides zero benefit - the d20 composition is sufficient for extracting signal from 200M tokens at 768 width. Simplicity criterion favors d20. Rules out: going deeper at same width is NOT a path forward. Future: either go wider with d20 or try different arch axis entirely.

ADAM_BETAS (0.8,0.95)->(0.9,0.95) at d20 (hypothesis: beta1=0.8 unusually low for AdamW, higher beta1=0.9 with more smoothing helps undertrained regime; evidence: ideas.tsv adam-betas-10 and nanochat autoresearch 'betas all messed up'): val_bpb 0.8988->0.9008 WORSE by +0.002. Mid-training losses tracked higher (step 767 loss 3.00 vs d20 ~2.9). Final train loss 2.660 similar. Conclusion: beta1=0.8 was correctly tuned - higher beta1 slows convergence by over-smoothing noisy early gradient signals. In undertrained regime each gradient step carries more unique information so LESS smoothing helps. Applies to embedding/lm_head/value_emb AdamW groups (Muon unchanged). Rules out: raising beta1 direction. Future: try LOWER beta1 or tune beta2.

x0_lambdas init 0.2->0.4 at d20 (hypothesis: 20 sequential layers dilute initial embedding x0 signal through depth so doubling broadcast init preserves input info evidence: x0_lambdas=0.2 was tuned at d16 d20 now has 25% more layers): val_bpb 0.8988->0.9034 WORSE by +0.0046. Final train loss 2.663 similar to d20. Conclusion: x0_lambda=0.2 init is correctly tuned even at d20 - the model self-corrects the learnable param during training but starting from higher init disrupts early optimization dynamics. The deeper d20 arch does NOT need more initial x0 broadcast. Rules out: x0 init tuning as a knob.

Multi-Latent Attention (MLA): factorize K,V through shared d_c=384 latent bottleneck (c_kv_down[768->384] + separate c_k_up/c_v_up[384->768]) replacing direct c_k/c_v projections at d20 (hypothesis: frees VRAM allowing larger batch/depth per DeepSeek-V2/V3 93% KV cache reduction; evidence: ideas.tsv mla-02): val_bpb 0.899->0.914 WORSE by +0.015. Train loss 2.685 vs d20 2.659 also worse. VRAM 9.2->9.2GB IDENTICAL (activation checkpointing dominates KV activations so MLA savings invisible). Conclusion: 384-dim latent bottleneck compresses K/V capacity too aggressively at 6x128 heads; compute-limited regime means we must SPEND VRAM savings (larger batch/deeper model) for MLA to help. Pure substitution at same model size hurts. Rules out: MLA without compensating capacity expansion.

Attention Residuals: softmax mix over all prior hidden states via (n_layer,n_layer+1) weight matrix + causal mask, replacing resid+x0 2-term mix [idea:attn-res-12] (hypothesis: generalizing 2-term residual to (d+1)-term softmax mix mitigates PreNorm dilution at d20; evidence: Kimi arxiv 2603.15031 + d20 keep ff77921 proves depth/signal is the lever): implementation infeasible with torch.compile+activation-checkpointing. Three attempts all failed: (1) unrolled Python sum: 3.85s/step (vs 3.2s baseline) due to compile fallback on dynamic loop bounds; (2) torch.stack+einsum: 5.1s/step; (3) per-block compile with eager outer: 12s/step due to launch overhead; (4) static-padded (21,B,T,C) tensor with additive scatter: OOM during backward (autograd retains all 20 intermediate padded tensors = ~5GB extra). Conclusion: at d20 the full softmax-mix-over-all-prior is too expensive in memory or compile-unfriendliness. A fixed-window variant (mix over last K=4 prior only) would be static-shape friendly and could be tried later. Rules out this specific full-all-prior formulation at d20.

GatedNorm: sigmoid(gate)*RMSNorm at all residual-stream norm sites (4 per regular block + 2 per mlp-only + 2 GPT-level = ~58k params init sigmoid(4)=0.982) (hypothesis: learnable gate mitigates residual sinks per arxiv 2601.22966 "Unified View of Attention/Residual Sinks" +2pt gain reported; evidence: plateau after d20 keep 5 consecutive non-improving exps): val_bpb 0.8988->0.8993 FLAT (+0.0005 noise). MFU dropped 73->68% due to sigmoid overhead in all norm calls. Train loss similar (2.655 vs 2.662). Conclusion: at d20/250M/200M-tokens our model does NOT suffer from residual sinks significantly enough for GatedNorm to help. The paper tested larger models where sinks are more pronounced. The 5% MFU penalty makes this strictly negative. Rules out: gating normalization layers at this scale.
